title: Parameters and Arguments
description: The difference between a parameter and an argument, multiple parameters, and Java's pass-by-value behavior.
objectives: Distinguish a parameter from an argument
Declare and use methods with multiple parameters
Understand that Java always passes primitive arguments by value

sections.text: These two words are often used interchangeably in casual conversation, but they mean slightly different things.

rows: The variable listed in the method's declaration
The actual value passed in when the method is called

sections.text: A variable declared in a method's signature that receives a value when the method is called.
The actual value supplied for a parameter at the point a method is called.
Arguments are matched to parameters by POSITION, not name. add(3, 4) sets a = 3 and b = 4 because 3 is first and a is first — not because of any name matching.

Recall widening from the Type Casting lesson: an int argument automatically widens to fit a double parameter, but not the reverse. Passing a double where an int parameter is expected requires an explicit cast, same as any narrowing conversion.

number still prints 5, NOT 100! When number is passed to tryToChange, Java copies its value into the parameter x. Changing x inside the method only changes that local copy — it has no effect on the original number back in main().
Java's parameter-passing behavior: a copy of the argument's value is given to the method, so changes to the parameter inside the method do not affect the original variable outside it.

This applies to primitives (int, double, boolean, etc.) exactly as shown. Reference types (like arrays and objects, previewed in Data Types and covered fully in Object-Oriented Programming) behave subtly differently — what's copied is the reference itself, which is a topic we return to once objects are properly introduced.
(a + b + c) / 3.0 uses the (double) division trick from Type Casting — dividing by 3.0 instead of 3 forces decimal division, so the average isn't truncated to a whole number.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming arguments are matched by name — they're matched strictly by position and type in the parameter list.
Expecting changes to a primitive parameter inside a method to affect the original variable passed in — pass-by-value copies the value, it doesn't share it.
Passing the wrong number or type of arguments — a method call must supply exactly as many arguments as there are parameters, each of a compatible type.
Confusing the terms parameter (in the declaration) and argument (at the call site) in interview answers.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
questions.answer: A parameter is the variable listed in a method's declaration. An argument is the actual value supplied for that parameter when the method is called.

questions.question: Does Java pass primitive arguments by value or by reference?
questions.answer: By value. A copy of the argument's value is assigned to the method's parameter, so modifying the parameter inside the method does not affect the original variable at the call site.

questions.question: How are multiple arguments matched to a method's parameters?
questions.answer: By position — the first argument fills the first parameter, the second fills the second, and so on — and each argument's type must be compatible with its corresponding parameter's type.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A parameter is the variable declared in a method's signature; an argument is the actual value passed in when the method is called, matched to parameters by position, not name. Java passes primitive arguments by value — the method receives a copy, so reassigning a parameter inside the method never affects the original variable back at the call site. Argument types must be compatible with their parameters' types, following the same widening/narrowing rules covered in Type Casting.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Parameter = the empty seat reserved in the method's signature. Argument = the passenger who actually sits there when you call it. Java hands over a PHOTOCOPY of that passenger, not the original.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What's the difference between a parameter and an argument?
If you change a parameter's value inside a method, does the original variable change too?
Are arguments matched to parameters by name or by position?
